Gen J N Chaudhuri

[Indian Army](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/indian-army/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Gen J N Chaudhuri

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0xg5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3096df1e-0e59-4b30-9bc1-06894e0f3f4a_220x355.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0xg5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3096df1e-0e59-4b30-9bc1-06894e0f3f4a_220x355.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

General Jayanto Nath Chaudhuri was one of India’s most pivotal military leaders, who commanded Operation Polo that integrated Hyderabad into India, later became the 5th Chief of Army Staff (1962–66), and steered the Army through the 1965 war with Pakistan before serving as High Commissioner to Canada. His career spanned combat in World War II, post-independence consolidation, and high-level diplomacy.

Born on **June 10, 1908, at Haripur**, Jayanto Nath Chaudhuri came from a lineage steeped in privilege, intellect, and cultural distinction. His paternal grandfather, **Durgadas Chaudhuri**, was the landlord of Chatmohar in present-day Bangladesh, while his grandmother, **Sukumari Devi**, was the sister of **Rabindranath Tagore**, placing him in direct kinship with Bengal’s greatest poet. His mother, **Pramila Chaudhuri**, was the daughter of **W.C. Banerjee**, the first President of the Indian National Congress, ensuring that the ideals of public service and national awakening were part of his inheritance.

Among his father’s elder brothers were men of rare accomplishment: **Sir Ashutosh Chaudhury**, a distinguished judge; **Prathamnath Chaudhuri**, a respected writer; and **Capt. Manmathanath Chaudhuri**, the first Indian Surgeon General of Madras Province. From this branch of the family came his first cousin, the legendary actress **Devika Rani**, whose artistry defined an era of Indian cinema.

After completing his studies at **St. Xavier’s College, Kolkata**, Jayanto Nath Chaudhuri entered the **Royal Military College, Sandhurst**, where his striking moustaches earned him the nickname *“Mucho.”* Among his batchmates was **Ayub Khan**, who would later become President of Pakistan, an ironic foreshadowing of the adversarial paths their nations would take.

Commissioned in 1928, he was first attached to the **1st Battalion of the North Staffordshire Regiment**, before joining the **7th Light Cavalry** in 1929. His early years were marked by discipline and adaptability, qualities that would define his career.

With the outbreak of **World War II**, Chaudhuri rose to the rank of acting Major and served in the **Western Desert campaigns** across Sudan, Eritrea, and Abyssinia as part of the **5th Indian Infantry Division**. In these harsh theatres of war, he distinguished himself for courage and tactical acumen. His gallantry was recognized with the award of the **Order of the British Empire (OBE)** in February 1943, a testament to his leadership in the Middle East.

Upon his recall to India, he was appointed **Senior Instructor at the Staff College, Quetta**, where he trained a new generation of officers in the art of modern warfare. This role reflected both his battlefield experience and his intellectual grasp of military science, positioning him as a bridge between combat and doctrine.

Once again, Chaudhuri found himself at the frontline, this time on the **Burma Front**, serving with the **16th Light Cavalry** from September 1944 to October 1945. In the dense jungles and unforgiving terrain of Burma, he displayed the same steadiness and distinction that had marked his desert campaigns.

With the end of the war, his rise was swift. Promoted to **Brigadier**, he was entrusted with command responsibilities in **British Malaya**, a region still recovering from Japanese occupation. His leadership there reflected both military discipline and administrative acumen.

As India stepped into independence in 1947, Chaudhuri’s expertise was called upon at the highest levels. He was appointed **Director of Military Operations & Intelligence** at Army Headquarters, a role critical to shaping the fledgling nation’s defense strategy. Working alongside **Major General Mohite**, he oversaw the complex and sensitive **military evacuation from Pakistan**, ensuring an orderly transfer of forces and assets during Partition.

The defining moment of General Jayanto Nath Chaudhuri’s career came in **September 1948**, when he was entrusted with leading **Operation Polo** as Commander of the **1st Armoured Division**. Tasked with integrating the princely state of Hyderabad into the Indian Union, Chaudhuri orchestrated a swift and decisive campaign that would alter the course of Indian history.

From the **Western Front at Solapur**, he directed a **two-pronged thrust** into Hyderabad, combining speed, firepower, and tactical ingenuity. To execute this plan, the Division was organized into four specialized task forces, each with a distinct role.

- **Strike Force** – a fast-moving spearhead of infantry, cavalry, and
  light artillery, designed to penetrate quickly and disrupt enemy
  defenses.

- **Smash Force** – the heavy armoured and artillery contingent, built
  to deliver crushing blows against fortified positions.

- **Kill Force** – infantry and engineering units, tasked with clearing
  resistance and securing captured ground.

- **Vir Force** – a versatile mix of infantry, anti-tank, and
  engineering units, ensuring resilience against counterattacks.

Under General Chaudhuri’s command, the Indian Army pressed forward with relentless precision. One of his major achievements was the **securing of Naldurg Fort near Solapur**, a strategic stronghold whose capture symbolized the collapse of Hyderabad’s defensive line. The advance continued through **Osmanabad and Aurangabad**, where the Army engaged in **intense street fighting**, clearing resistance block by block.

The momentum was unstoppable. On **September 17, 1948**, Indian forces entered **Bidar** (now in Karnataka), while another column seized **Chityal** in Nalgonda district, barely 60 kilometers from Hyderabad city itself. Simultaneously, **Hingoli in Maharashtra** fell, tightening the noose around the Nizam’s dominion.

The Hyderabad State Army was utterly routed. In the brief but decisive campaign, they suffered **490 dead, 122 wounded, and 1,647 taken prisoner**. The **Razakars**, the paramilitary force that had vowed to defend the Nizam’s dream of independence, fared even worse—**1,373 were killed and 1,911 captured**, their ambitions collapsing in the face of India’s coordinated assault.

Realizing the futility of further resistance, the **Nizam announced a ceasefire at 5 PM IST on September 17, 1948**. He disbanded the Razakars and permitted the Indian Army’s entry into Hyderabad. The following day, **September 18**, the Hyderabad Commander-in-Chief, **Major General El Edroos**, formally surrendered to **General J.N. Chaudhuri**, sealing the fate of the princely state. Soon after, **Qasim Rizvi**, the Razakar leader, was arrested by the Indian Government.

With this, Hyderabad’s bid for independence ended, and its integration into the Indian Union was complete. Chaudhuri’s leadership.swift, decisive, and disciplined, had ensured that Operation Polo would be remembered as one of the most efficient military campaigns in modern Indian history.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ykAa!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4817953c-edb6-4230-973b-98f3c617231d_200x242.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ykAa!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4817953c-edb6-4230-973b-98f3c617231d_200x242.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8K8v!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F96cf36fe-9fbb-4240-a0db-b53277df41ae_200x328.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8K8v!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F96cf36fe-9fbb-4240-a0db-b53277df41ae_200x328.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

After the swift success of **Operation Polo**, Chaudhuri’s responsibilities extended beyond the battlefield. He was appointed **Military Governor of Hyderabad State**, overseeing the delicate transition from princely rule to democratic governance. His administrative acumen ensured stability during a politically sensitive period.

In **1949**, he was named the first **Colonel Commandant of the Corps of Electrical & Mechanical Engineers (EME)**, a newly created branch vital to modernizing India’s military infrastructure. This appointment reflected his forward-looking vision, recognizing the importance of technology and engineering in sustaining a modern army.

By **January 1952**, Chaudhuri had risen to the post of **Adjutant General at Army Headquarters**, holding the substantive rank of Major-General. In this role, he was responsible for personnel management, discipline, and welfare across the Army—an assignment that demanded both organizational skill and human sensitivity.

The following year, in **January 1953**, he was appointed **Chief of the General Staff**, placing him at the very heart of India’s military planning and operations. This position marked him as one of the most trusted strategists of the post-independence era, bridging the lessons of colonial campaigns with the demands of a sovereign nation’s defense.

The debacle of the **1962 Sino-Indian War** forced a reckoning within the Army. The then Chief of Army Staff, General Pran Nath Thapar, stepped down, and in **February 1963**, Jayanto Nath Chaudhuri was appointed as the **5th Chief of Army Staff**. His tenure was defined by the urgent task of rebuilding morale, restoring confidence, and modernizing the Army after the trauma of defeat.

One of his most enduring contributions was his vision for a dedicated paramilitary force to guard India’s borders during peacetime. He proposed the creation of a specialized organization that could relieve the Army of routine border duties and allow it to focus on war preparedness. This idea took shape as the **Border Security Force (BSF)**, established in 1965, with Chaudhuri rightly remembered as one of its founding fathers.

As **Chief of Army Staff**, General Chaudhuri carried the immense responsibility of leading India through the **Indo-Pak War of 1965**. While the Army held its ground and the war ended without territorial loss, his leadership during the conflict has been the subject of considerable debate.

One of the major criticisms was the **lack of coordination between the Army and the Indian Air Force (IAF)**. Chaudhuri, by temperament and training, often viewed the IAF and Navy as auxiliary arms of the Army rather than independent services with their own strategic doctrines. This outlook led to friction and missed opportunities for joint operations.

His **unilateral style of decision-making** compounded the issue. Plans were often kept close to his chest, with limited consultation even among senior commanders. While this approach reflected his instinct for control and clarity, it alienated colleagues and created gaps in communication at critical moments.

One of the most debated episodes was his assessment of India’s logistical position. He advised Prime Minister **Lal Bahadur Shastri** that the Army was running dangerously low on stocks and supplies, a report that weighed heavily in Shastri’s decision to accept a ceasefire.

In reality, India’s reserves were far healthier than Chaudhuri’s assessment suggested, and the **Pakistan Army had suffered severe losses**. Many later argued that India could have pressed its advantage further. This misjudgment, combined with his **unilateral style of decision-making** and strained relations with the Air Force and Navy, cast a shadow over his wartime leadership.

For his distinguished service to the nation, General Chaudhuri was awarded the **Padma Vibhushan**, one of India’s highest civilian honors. In **1966**, after **38 years of service**, he retired from the Army, leaving behind a legacy of both triumphs and controversies, but above all of dedication to India’s defense.

Post-retirement, he continued to serve the nation as **High Commissioner to Canada (1966–1969)**, strengthening India’s diplomatic presence abroad. In 1979, he became the **first Indian Army Chief to write an autobiography**, offering rare insights into the life of a soldier-statesman. Away from the battlefield and corridors of power, he was known as a **lover of Western classical music**, a reminder of the cultured upbringing that had shaped him.

General Chaudhuri passed away on **April 6, 1983**, in New Delhi. His memory endures not only in military history but also in tradition: the **Border Security Force annually awards the General J. N. Chaudhuri Trophy** in his name, honoring his role as one of the founding fathers of the BSF.

Sources

https://www.firstpost.com/india/how-our-generals-repeatedly-bungled-during-the-1965-war-but-junior-commanders-saved-the-day-for-us-10117031.html
